questions.question: According to Newton's First Law of Motion, an object at rest tends to stay at rest unless acted upon by a net external force. Give an example from everyday life that supports this law.
questions.studentAnswer: A book placed on a table remains at rest until someone pushes it. This shows that an object at rest stays at rest unless acted upon by an external force.

questions.question: What is the relationship between acceleration and net force? Use Newton's Second Law of Motion in your answer.
questions.studentAnswer: According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, acceleration is directly proportional to the net force and inversely proportional to mass (F = ma). This means greater force produces greater acceleration for a given mass.
questions.feedback: Good job! You correctly explained Newton's Second Law of Motion and its relationship to acceleration and mass.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from rest, reaching a speed of 30 m/s in 10 seconds. Calculate its average acceleration.
questions.studentAnswer: Initial velocity (u) = 0, final velocity (v) = 30 m/s, time (t) = 10 s. Acceleration (a) = (v - u) / t = 30 / 10 = 3 m/s^2.
questions.feedback: Well done! Your answer is correct and shows good understanding of Newton's Second Law.

questions.question: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in motion. Explain why it is easier to keep a moving ball rolling than to start it rolling from rest. Consider four different objects: a feather, a marble, a brick and a bowling ball. Each of these objects has been dropped from a height on Earth. Assume that the only force acting on each object is gravity. You are given the following information:
questions.studentAnswer: It is easier to keep a moving ball rolling than to start it because of inertia. An object at rest resists motion, while a moving object tends to continue moving. Heavier objects like a bowling ball have more inertia than lighter ones like a feather, so they resist changes in motion more strongly.

questions.question: A ladder 10 m long leans against a wall such that its base is 6 m from the wall. What is the height of the top of the ladder above the ground?
questions.studentAnswer:
Mathematics — Pythagorean Theorem
Topic: Pythagoras Theorem | Difficulty: Medium
Student: Sample Student | Date: 04/04/2026 | Total Marks: 20
Q1. A ladder 10 m long leans against a wall such that its base is 6 m from the wall.
What is the height of the top of the ladder above the ground?
[4 marks]
Working:
• Using the Pythagorean theorem: a
2
 + b
2
 = c
2
• Here, the ladder is the hypotenuse (c = 10 m) and the base is one leg (b = 6 m).
• So: h
2
 + 6
2
 = 10
2
• h
2
 + 36 = 100
• h
2
 = 100 - 36 = 64
• h = sqrt(64) = 8 m
Answer: The height of the top of the ladder above the ground is 8 metres.
Q2. A right triangle has sides a = 7 cm and b = 9 cm. Using the Pythagorean
theorem, find the length of c, which is the hypotenuse.
[4 marks]
Working:
• Using: c
2
 = a
2
 + b
2
• c
2
 = 7
2
 + 9
2
• c
2
 = 49 + 81 = 130
• c = sqrt(130) = approximately 11.40 cm
Answer: The hypotenuse c is approximately 11.40 cm.
Q3. A car travels 60 km/h for 2 hours, then 80 km/h for 3 hours. What is its average
speed during this time?
[4 marks]
Working:
• Distance in first part: 60 km/h x 2 h = 120 km
• Distance in second part: 80 km/h x 3 h = 240 km
• Total distance: 120 + 240 = 360 km
• Total time: 2 + 3 = 5 hours
• Average speed = Total distance / Total time = 360 / 5 = 72 km/h
Answer: The average speed of the car is 72 km/h.

Q4. In a rectangle, the diagonal measures 15 cm and one side measures 12 cm. What
is the length of the other side?
[4 marks]
Working:
• The diagonal of a rectangle forms a right triangle with the two sides.
• Using: diagonal
2
 = side1
2
 + side2
2
• 15
2
 = 12
2
 + side2
2
• 225 = 144 + side2
2
• side2
2
 = 225 - 144 = 81
• side2 = sqrt(81) = 9 cm
Answer: The length of the other side of the rectangle is 9 cm.
Q5. A right triangle has sides x = 6 cm and y = 8 cm. Find the measure of angle theta
opposite to the shorter leg x, in degrees.
[4 marks]
Working:
• First, find the hypotenuse: c
2
 = 6
2
 + 8
2
 = 36 + 64 = 100, so c = 10 cm.
• The angle theta is opposite to x = 6 cm (the shorter leg).
• Using sine: sin(theta) = opposite / hypotenuse = 6 / 10 = 0.6
• theta = arcsin(0.6) = approximately 36.87 degrees
Answer: The angle theta opposite to the shorter leg is approximately 36.87 degrees.
— End of Answer Sheet —

questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of electromagnetic induction?
questions.studentAnswer: Electricity and magnetism are two different phenomena.
questions.feedback: Please include a complete answer with correct information about electromagnetic induction.
questions.correctAnswer: Electromagnetic radiation can be used to create heat.

questions.question: What is the equation for calculating magnetic field strength?
questions.studentAnswer: B = 4uH/2
questions.feedback: The correct equation for calculating magnetic field strength is B = uH/4

questions.question: Which type of magnet is stronger: bar magnets or electromagnets?
questions.studentAnswer: Bar magnets are always stronger than electromagnets.
questions.correctAnswer: Both types of magnets have the same strength.

questions.question: What is an electromagnetic wave?
questions.studentAnswer: A wave that carries both electric and magnetic fields.
questions.feedback: Correct! An electromagnetic wave is a type of wave that consists of changing electric and magnetic fields.
questions.correctAnswer: A wave that carries both electric and magnetic fields.

questions.question: What is an electric field?
questions.studentAnswer: A region of space where there are more negative charges than positive charges.
questions.feedback: Your answer is not entirely correct as an electric field is actually a type of electromagnetic radiation, like light or radio waves. However, you have partially answered the question correctly by mentioning that it's related to electromagnetic radiation.

questions.question: What is a magnetic dipole?
questions.studentAnswer: Two magnets with opposite poles facing each other.
